# Commonwealth Bank gives ugly inflation warning amid hope RBA could 'hold the line'

A major reveal on Wednesday could be the deciding factor for the RBA's interest rate decision next week.

[![Nick Whigham](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/IlOqiaQDnETNL6WPSAgM_A--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTgwO2g9ODA7Y2Y9d2VicA--/https://s.yimg.com/os/creatr-uploaded-images/2025-10/706f0c20-a3d6-11f0-bfdf-b46ad89eecce)](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/author/nick-whigham)

[Nick Whigham](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/author/nick-whigham)· Head of Yahoo Finance Australia

Updated Mon 27 April 2026 at 2:53 am GMT+14 min read

- [CBA.AX \\
  -0.81%](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/CBA.AX/ "CBA.AX")


![Commonwealth Bank branch in Australia. ](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/Pv_gzu4C7WyJvD6u6ZZi9w--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTk2MDtoPTY0MDtjZj13ZWJw/https://s.yimg.com/os/creatr-uploaded-images/2026-04/bfead430-41da-11f1-94b9-e8392018f13a)

CBA has tipped inflation to rise almost a full percentage point thanks to the Iran war. (Source: Getty)·AFP via Getty Images

The Reserve Bank of Australia is facing an incredibly difficult call. The [Board meets next week](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/rba-interest-rate-decision-big-banks-predict-painful-blow-not-seen-in-three-years-as-possible-2026-lifeline-identified-224418108.html) amid continued uncertainty over the war in Iran, and a week out from a Federal Budget [expected to contain some big changes](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/financial-advisors-brace-for-major-property-tax-changes-as-popular-wealth-strategy-becomes-less-powerful-224739772.html). Against that backdrop, it is expected [to slug mortgage holders and businesses](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/rba-governor-michele-bullock-warns-of-costly-scenario-for-australia-it-will-be-much-worse-044102150.html) with a hike in the official cash rate.

But borrowers could – and should – be spared another blow, according to some prognosticators going against the grain. As house prices in major cities are rolling over, certain economic commentators think the RBA should stand pat.

A hike would be the third in a row, but the second since surging fuel prices took hold.

"Because that interest rate increase — or the equivalent — has already come through in higher petrol prices, I reckon they might hold the line," said David Koch.

**RELATED**

- [**ATO reveals 'focus areas' as workers warned over common claims**](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/ato-reveals-focus-areas-for-end-of-financial-year-tax-returns-as-workers-warned-over-common-claims-000448788.html)

- [**Federal budget speculation over return of tax not seen for nearly 50 years**](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/federal-budget-speculation-over-return-of-tax-not-seen-in-australia-for-nearly-50-years-224656290.html)

- [**The decade that can make or break your retirement: 'It's critical'**](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/aussies-warned-over-the-decade-that-can-make-or-break-your-retirement-its-critical-054510881.html)


The Economic Director at Compare the Market, and regular _Yahoo Finance_ [contributor](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/author/david-koch/), believes the bank could wait for at least some of the dust to settle and see what's in the Federal Budget on May 12.

"They’ll be thinking about whether oil prices will stay high for longer, because if the Middle East crisis resolves itself, oil prices will drop significantly — and that would take a big chunk out of the inflation rate," he said.

He also pointed to deteriorating conditions in the economy [and historically glum consumer sentiment](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/calls-for-millions-of-aussie-workers-to-get-2465-pay-rise-as-inflation-expectations-hit-record-high-212637523.html) as factors that could reduce demand that caused inflation to tick back up this year in Australia's productivity constrained economy.

"Consumer confidence has plunged and business confidence has fallen to almost record lows. Consumers cutting their spending is bad for the economy because small businesses start to suffer.

"And bosses not having confidence is bad for the economy too, because they won’t invest and they won’t hire people. So the Reserve Bank doesn’t want to crush consumers and businesses with another interest rate increase," he said.

The ANZAC Day weekend brought another soft result in auction clearance rates in the country's biggest housing markets (with Adelaide being a notable exception). In Sydney, auction clearance rates on Saturday were 49 per cent (compared to 63 per cent a year ago) and in Melbourne was 56 per cent (down from 61 per cent the same time last year), according to Domain.

Economist and former advisor to the Gillard government, Stephen Koukoulas, also believes the right move is not to hike, [and says a softening housing market could play a part](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/the-obvious-mistake-most-home-buyers-are-making-right-now-hesitation-turns-to-urgency-020020224.html) in a surprise decision to hold.

Story continues

"The loss of momentum in house prices and outright falls now in Sydney and Melbourne is particularly interesting," he said on Monday.

![RBA governor Michele Bullock and Australian housing.](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Despite the high probability of a hike, the RBA will have an incredibly tough call to make next week. (Source: Getty/Yahoo Finance)

While he noted the RBA doesn't target house prices, the overall impact they have on the economy and sentiment is relevant.

"Given the importance of housing for household wealth and the efficient functioning of the banking system, house price falls will start to feature prominently in RBA thinking," he claimed.

The frequently dovish economist, last week said lifting interest rates further into the already tough conditions for households "would be monetary policy vandalism".

## Impact of fuel crisis on Aussie inflation to be revealed on Wednesday

Ultimately, it could come down to a data point to be unveiled on Wednesday. The first set of inflation numbers since US and Israeli strikes on Iran led to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and skyrocketing oil prices will be unveiled on Wednesday.

A large inflation spike in March is expected by economists, off the back of sharp increases to petrol and diesel prices which have filtered through to many sectors of the economy.

Commonwealth Bank economists have tipped inflation to rise almost a full percentage point for the month, from 3.7 per cent to 4.6 per cent.

The trimmed mean inflation, which removes the more volatile price increases such as petrol costs, will nudge up from 3.3 per cent to 3.5 per cent, they forecast.

CBA economists, in unison with the other Big Four banks, said the RBA was more likely than not to hike rates again off the back of Wednesday's inflation data.

"The data will be keenly watched given the RBA is due to hand down its decision on interest rates on May 5, with money markets suggesting a 72 per cent chance of a 25 basis point rate hike," the bank said.

The Reserve Bank favours the trimmed mean as a true measure of inflation, with the figures already well above the bank's target band of between two per cent and three per cent.

**with AAP**

# Australia’s RBA raises rates in split decision as inflation fears intensify

Story by James Glynn

• 1mo
•

3 min read

In this article

![ROYAL BANK OF CANADA](https://bing.com/th?id=OSK.08d43967697f35980c2380d60b9daf13)RY▲‎+0.39%‎

See details

![BANK OF JAPAN](https://bing.com/th?id=OSK.857165bce08f8d08d4cf76e8bf82ebaa)8301Dropping fast

See details

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

SYDNEY—Australia’s central bank raised interest rates on Tuesday as the conflict in Iran worsened existing concerns around an acceleration in inflation and the country’s reliance on oil imports.

The Reserve Bank of Australia increased the official cash rate to 4.10% from 3.85%. That marked the second increase this year and investor bets have shifted toward another rate rise in May as officials battle to rein in price pressures.

“The board judged that inflation is likely to remain above target for some time and that the risks have tilted further to the upside, including to inflation expectations. It was therefore appropriate to increase the cash rate target,” the RBA said in a statement.

Central banks worldwide are signaling a rethink in their approach to interest rates as the conflict in the Middle East drives up energy prices and disrupts global trade in everything from cars to playdough. Some airports in the Middle East are closed. Major container-shipping companies including A.P. Moller-Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd have suspended key routes in and out of the Middle East on safety grounds.

“Developments in the Middle East remain highly uncertain, but under a wide range of possible scenarios could add to global and domestic inflation,” the RBA board added.

The decision to increase interest rates was a split decision, with five of the nine board members voting for an increase.

Among central banks due to decide on interest rates this week are the Federal Reserve and the Bank of Canada, which will announce the outcome of their latest policy meetings on Wednesday. The European Central Bank, the Bank of Japan and the Bank of England are also scheduled to announce interest-rate decisions in the coming days.

For central banks, decisions likely boil down to whether they view the inflationary impact of the Middle East conflict as fleeting or whether it will cause a more sustained bout of price pressures. They also need to assess whether the jolt to demand is big enough to offset much of the economic pain.

Energy prices have surged on the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, the small but globally significant waterway between Iran and Oman. Brent crude oil-the global benchmark-is up 65% this year and hit a 12-month closing high of $103.14 a barrel last week.

Australia and much of Asia are exposed to an energy price shock because they rely on imports of crude oil and products such as gasoline and jet fuel.

Already, many governments are taking action to cushion the impact of high energy prices. Australia has released some of the oil it had stocked away for use in an emergency and temporarily lowered fuel standards, meaning that more-polluting fuel can be sold to consumers.

With no certainty around how long the war will last and the challenge in smoothing supply chains following any cease-fire, many central banks are either edging toward pausing plans to cut interest rates more or, like Australia, raising borrowing costs to prevent inflation running hot.

Australia’s inflation rate was already at 3.8% on year before Israel and the U.S. launched the military campaign against Iran. That compares to the central bank’s target range of 2%-3%.

“I’ve made it really clear that we had an inflation challenge in our economy and developments in the Middle East over the last couple of weeks will make that more challenging rather than less challenging,” Treasurer Jim Chalmers said in an interview with Channel 9 last week.

A former head of the RBA’s economic analysis department, John Simon, said this week that he expects inflation to approach 5.0% in annual terms, warning that there is scope for even higher numbers if the conflict in the Middle East lasts longer than expected.

Australia’s economy is growing solidly, but it is also grinding up capacity constraints which has reignited inflation since the middle of last year. A decade of weak productivity growth has worsened the central bank’s woes.

Rising interest rates are heaping pressure on the government to cut spending while also offering new support to households in the government’s budget in May.

“The challenge is that some of the economics tools previously used to manage ‘cost of living’ challenges actually added to demand and inflation problem,” said Paul Bloxham, HSBC’s chief economist for Australia and New Zealand.

# The Aldi shrinkage issue customers complain about, and we don't blame them

Story by Laura Oakley

• 4h
•

3 min read

In this article

![REDDIT, INC.](https://bing.com/th?id=OSK.71cb5e32cdfc96b404b60d4a5e4d5621)RDDTRising fast

See details

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

Shrinkflation. It's a buzzword that you've probably heard a lot of over the past few years. It's when companies shrink the size or portion of a consumable household product (like food, or toilet paper) without conversely lowering the price. In fact, in some cases, the cost actually increases. Hence, consumers are getting less for more. It seems to have gotten bad in the world of groceries — and customers have definitely noticedd the size differences. Some Aldi shoppers have taken to Reddit to share their woes about shrunk portions of their favorite products.

In a [Reddit post](https://www.reddit.com/r/aldi/comments/1cxaf55/shrinkflation_at_aldi/), an Aldi shopper provided a photo of two bags of Clancy's nacho cheese-flavored tortilla chips side by side, with the comment: "This bag was $2.09 for 11 ounces a few days ago, and today it is $2.39 for 9 ounces." In another example, a [Reddit post](https://www.reddit.com/r/aldi/comments/1rzrm0m/shrinkflation_even_aldi_isnt_immune/) titled "Shrinkflation, even Aldi isn't immune" shows Lacura Sparkling Lemon hand soap shrunk from 10.14 to 8.75 fluid ounces, sold at the same price. The author asked if anyone else had noticed this trend. Several people weighed in, reporting on everything from orange juice to Seasons Choice frozen produce and Greek yogurt.

Among the [15 facts about Aldi you should know](https://www.tastingtable.com/1839572/facts-about-aldi/) is that they use unconventional methods to cut costs, including having [minimal employees](https://www.tastingtable.com/2137659/why-aldi-employees-arent-seen-much-short-staffed/). Even [the most annoying things about shopping at Aldi](https://www.tastingtable.com/1942913/worst-annoying-things-shopping-aldi/), like providing a returnable quarter for a shopping cart, are all about keeping prices low for customers. So, with all these cost-cutting measures already in place, why does Aldi participate in shrinkflation?

Read more: [16 Aldi Breads, Ranked Worst To Best](https://www.tastingtable.com/1829497/aldi-bread-ranked-worst-best/?zsource=msnsyndicated)

## Why does shrinkflation happen?

Shrinkflation isn't exactly a brand-new phenomenon. The U.S. Government Accountability Office (GAO) has been studying product downsizing, and an [August 2025 report](https://www.gao.gov/blog/what-shrinkflation-and-how-has-it-affected-grocery-store-items-recently) stated that, over the past 10 years, it occurred most frequently in 2015 and increased again in 2022. To answer the question of why shrinkflation happens: When production costs increase, manufacturers have a decision to make on whether to increase prices, shrink the size, or, as we've seen, a combination of both. In a review of studies conducted by the GAO, customers were "less likely to react to downsizing than to price increases."

While annoying, customers seem to be loyal to their brands and will still purchase items even after they've been downsized. A [2014 study](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0022435913000456) published in the Journal of Retailing even found that consumers are approximately four times more sensitive to price than to package size, and that shrinking product size can maintain or increase profitability for companies. Aldi is as susceptible to increasing production costs as much as any other U.S. grocer; hence, it's not above shrinkflation in order to stay profitable.

To increase transparency, some states are changing the way products are labeled so that a per-unit price is apparent to the consumer. This may help them detect when a product has shrunk while the price remains the same. Other ideas include downsizing labels and disclosure on packaging, banning deceptive downsizing practices, and more education around the topic. In the meantime, Reddit seems to be the place to compare notes and stay vigilant regarding shrinkflation.

# Asia week ahead: Japan rate decision and key data from China and Australia

The Bank of Japan will announce a hotly-debated interest rate decision. Key data releases include China's purchasing managers’ index, Australian inflation and Taiwanese GDP

Authors


[![Deepali Bhargava](https://think.ing.com/uploads/authors/_w160h160/Dheepali_-_white_background.jpg)\\
\\

![120118-image-_asia.jpg](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/)

In this Article

[Asia Research highlights of the week](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/#a1) [Japan: Inflation and labour market data complicate rate decision](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/#a2) [South Korea: Exports to show strength](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/#a3) [China: PMI data could see a contraction](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/#a4) [Taiwan: We expect continued growth in Q1](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/#a5) [Australia: Inflation to reflect oil price surge](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-week-ahead-240426/#a6)

#### Asia Research highlights of the week

[South Korean GDP surges, but K-shaped growth is challenge for central bank](https://think.ing.com/snaps/korean-gdp-surged-with-an-expected-slowdown-2026-growth-outlook-is-now-higher/)

[Japanese inflation quickened in March, complicating Bank of Japan outlook](https://think.ing.com/snaps/japanese-inflation-growth-quickened-in-march/)

[The Philippines kicks off hiking cycle, reasserting inflation control amid rising oil prices](https://think.ing.com/articles/philippines-kicks-off-hiking-cycle-reasserting-inflation-control-amid-rising-oil-prices/)

[Bank Indonesia holds rates, prioritises rupiah stability](https://think.ing.com/articles/bank-indonesia-holds-rates-prioritises-rupiah-stability/)

[Asia FX Talking: North-south divide](https://think.ing.com/articles/asia-fx-talking-april-2026/)

#### Japan: Inflation and labour market data complicate rate decision

Japanese data should indicate that inflationary pressures are intensifying, while the economy remains resilient. We expect labour market conditions to remain tight with the jobless rate edging down to 2.5%. Monthly activity data should rebound quite firmly, partially offsetting the previous month’s declines. We don’t think the energy shock has had a significant negative impact on production so far. Meanwhile, the Tokyo CPI is expected to rise faster in April, reflecting recent energy price hikes, a weak JPY, solid wage growth, and bi-annual price adjustments.

These developments complicate the Bank of Japan’s rate decision on Tuesday. Markets widely expect the BoJ to stay on hold amid uncertainty surrounding the Middle East situation. We continue to believe there’s a chance that the BoJ may hike on 28 April. Board members’ concerns about higher inflation expectations are likely to increase as real interest rates remain deeply negative. CPI inflation accelerated more than expected in March. It’s set to accelerate further in the coming months. The BoJ will release its quarterly outlook report on rate-decision day. The inflation outlook is likely to be revised upward from the current 1.9% to 2.4% for fiscal year 2026 and 2.0% to 2.2% for FY2027. Meanwhile, the GDP outlook is expected to be revised down modestly from the current 1.0% to 0.7% for FY2026. Even with the downward revision, GDP is expected to remain above potential, which should support BoJ rate hikes.

#### South Korea: Exports to show strength

Early April trade data shows robust chip exports are driving Korean export growth. With memory chip prices climbing and refineries passing higher input costs through to output, we expect April exports to increase 50% year over year.

#### China: PMI data could see a contraction

China releases its April PMI data. The official PMI rebounded to expansionary territory in March. In April, we could see the index dip back into contractionary territory, with the manufacturing PMI falling to 49.9 and the non-manufacturing PMI dipping to 49.8. We’re likely to see evidence of price pressures continuing to build in the PMI sub-indices.

#### Taiwan: We expect continued growth in Q1

Taiwan releases its first-quarter GDP data on Thursday. We expect Taiwan’s stellar growth to continue, with 11.5% YoY in the January-March period amid consistently outperforming data so far this year. Growth will be primarily driven by external demand again. There’s been a flurry of upward revisions over the last two years, as the economy continues to exceed expectations at nearly every turn. We revised our full-year GDP forecast up to 8.2% YoY from 6.7% YoY earlier this month. The first quarter release will go a long way to showing if this is an overshoot or still too conservative.

#### Australia: Inflation to reflect oil price surge

Australia's CPI is expected to accelerate to 4.6% YoY, largely driven by the surge in oil prices. Inflation dynamics are likely to deteriorate further in the second quarter amid ongoing geopolitical tensions and no meaningful de‑escalation in the war. If the Reserve Bank of Australia intends to stay ahead of the curve, it’s likely to move in May rather than waiting until August.

# Westpac lifts fixed mortgage rates again

Canstar data shows fixed-rate hikes spreading across most lenders as sub-6% deals thin out


![Westpac lifts fixed mortgage rates again](https://cdn-res.keymedia.com/cdn-cgi/image/w=1000,h=600,f=auto/https://cdn-res.keymedia.com/cms/images/au/061/0314_637428992934083789.jpg)

By
[Rommel Lontayao](https://www.mpamag.com/au/authors/rommel-lontayao)

24 Apr 2026

Share

![facebook sharing button](https://platform-cdn.sharethis.com/img/facebook.svg)

![twitter sharing button](https://platform-cdn.sharethis.com/img/twitter.svg)

![linkedin sharing button](https://platform-cdn.sharethis.com/img/linkedin.svg)

[Westpac](https://www.mpamag.com/au/companies/westpac/308084) has increased its fixed home loan rates for the second time in three weeks, adding 0.15 percentage points to selected products as markets weigh the chance of further Reserve Bank tightening, with the next decision due in 12 days.

The move follows Westpac’s earlier increases of up to 0.45 percentage points. After the latest change, the bank’s lowest advertised fixed rate is 6.29% on a two-year term. Despite the rise, Westpac remains the cheapest of the major banks on fixed rates.

| Big four banks’ lowest fixed rates |
| --- |
| Term | [CBA](https://www.mpamag.com/au/companies/cba/308061) | Westpac | [NAB](https://www.mpamag.com/au/companies/nab/421631) | [ANZ](https://www.mpamag.com/au/companies/anz/300467) |
| 1-year | 6.49% | 6.39% | 6.34% | 6.34% |
| 2-year | 6.34% | 6.29% | 6.39% | 6.39% |
| 3-year | 6.59% | 6.49% | 6.49% | 6.54% |
| 4-year | 6.64% | 6.69% | 6.49% | 6.54% |
| 5-year | 6.79% | 6.69% | 6.49% | 6.59% |
| Source: Canstar.com.au. Rates based on owner-occupier fixed rate loans. LVR requirements apply. |

Canstar’s rate tracking indicates the repricing is widespread. Its figures show more than 90% of lenders have lifted at least one fixed rate since the RBA’s March cash rate decision, including all four major banks. Two of the majors have raised fixed rates twice over that period. Since 17 March, the banks have adjusted fixed rates on the following dates: [Commonwealth Bank](https://www.mpamag.com/au/companies/commonwealth-bank/421613) on 27 March; Westpac on 2 April and 23 April; NAB on 27 March and 10 April; and ANZ on 1 April.

Canstar’s research suggests lenders may be building in expectations of more than a single RBA increase. The average of the lowest fixed rates on its database has risen by 0.42 to 0.43 percentage points over the past five weeks.

The number of lenders offering at least one fixed rate below 6% has fallen to 19, compared with 83 at the start of the year, according to Canstar. The lowest fixed rate listed is 5.49% from Northern Inland Credit Union for a one-year term.

| Lowest fixed rates |
| --- |
| Term | Lender | Lowest rate from |
| 1-year | Northern Inland Credit Union | 5.49% |
| 2-year | Bank Vic | 5.79% |
| 3-year | [BankVic](https://www.mpamag.com/au/companies/bankvic/547629) | 5.79% |
| 4-year | BankVic | 6.09% |
| 5-year | Horizon Bank | 5.94% |
| Source: Source: Canstar.com.au. Rates based on owner-occupier fixed rate loans. LVR requirements apply. |

![Sally Tindall of Canstar.com.au](https://cdn-res.keymedia.com/cms/images/us/075/0376_639125310143785961.png)“Fixed rates are on the move again, with some lenders going for a second round of hikes in-between RBA meetings,” said Sally Tindall (pictured right), data insights director at Canstar.com.au.

“Westpac’s doubling down on fixed rate hikes isn't an isolated event. Plenty of banks are heading back to the pricing drawing board, some for the second time in a matter of weeks, as a result of growing concern Australia’s inflation rate is about to spike once again.

“Canstar analysis shows more than 90% of lenders have moved fixed rates since the [last RBA decision](https://www.mpamag.com/au/news/general/rba-raises-cash-rate-to-41/568740), including all four big banks, with both Westpac and NAB having two stabs at it in this time.”

According to Canstar, lenders are not just hiking by a standard cash rate increase, with average owner-occupier fixed rate having risen by 0.43 percentage points since the last RBA decision in March.

“Banks don’t exactly want to get caught short having to fund what might turn out to be a competitively-priced fixed rate loan, particularly when a rate that starts with a ‘6’ isn’t going to help them attract new customers,” Tindall said.

“For borrowers, this swift repricing is a clear signal – opportunities to lock in a more competitive fixed rate are slipping away.

“There’s just 19 lenders offering fixed rates below 6% today. At the start of the year, it was about the same number of lenders offering [rates below 5%](https://www.mpamag.com/au/news/general/say-goodbye-to-sub-5-mortgages-if-rba-lifts-cash-rate-today/563909). That’s a sharp about-face in the fixed-rate market in a remarkably short space of time.”

# The RBA is likely to hike rates in May but war-driven inflation yet to peak

## Economists think the Reserve Bank will increase rates in May following surging fuel prices. But as the Middle East conflict drags on, the worst of the inflation effects may be yet to come.

![](https://static.capitalbrief.com/assets/6d0a055c-9506-4e4b-aa1f-219017ef4e2c/1690414263/?key=img-author-sm)

[Jennifer Duke](https://www.capitalbrief.com/author/jennifer-duke/)4:46pm yesterday

[![](https://assets.capitalbrief.com/img/icon-google.png)Make us a preferred sourceMake us a preferred source on Google](https://www.google.com/preferences/source?q=capitalbrief.com)

![](https://static.capitalbrief.com/assets/4883d8ea-68eb-4fba-af2c-2b8694cbee7c/1777175147/?key=img-lead)
Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock has been carefully weighing the hit to the economy with the hit to inflation from the US-Iran war.
AAP Image/Dan Himbrechts.

The final data this week ahead of the May rate decision will make-or-break economists’ predictions for the cash rate. Regardless, top forecasters are warning that the inflation impacts from the Middle East conflict are yet to appear in the data.

Here’s what you need to know about the key economic data releases this week.

### New numbers

**The data: Consumer price index, March 2026, Australian Bureau of Statistics**

**When:** Wednesday 29 April 2026

![](https://static.capitalbrief.com/assets/d8ff3a1d-f6f6-40b2-9efe-95887bb4f763?key=img-article)

**What to expect:** With only a week until the **Reserve Bank** board makes its next interest rate decision, economists are focused on what this week’s consumer price index reveals about price pressures, particularly as the March data captures the start of the US-Iran conflict.

The market is expecting to see year-on-year growth in headline CPI for March at 4.8% and the trimmed mean at 3.4%. On a quarterly basis, the market is expected to see headline growth up 1.4% and the trimmed mean up 0.9%, or about 3.5% over the year.

# Asia’s Big Week: China PMIs, BoJ, And RBA Inflation Test

A packed run of data and rate decisions from China, Japan, and Australia will set the tone for Asian markets as trade, pricing, and energy risks stay in focus.

![Asia’s Big Week: China PMIs, BoJ, And RBA Inflation Test](https://finimize.com/_next/image?url=https%3A%2F%2Ffinimize-img.imgix.net%2Fhttps%253A%252F%252Fchivas-assets.s3-eu-west-1.amazonaws.com%252Fstatic%252Fimages%252FG2637652_xml.jpeg%3Fixlib%3Dpython-3.1.2%26s%3Dc35741c50ff28d22b6a2a014233c2b1e&w=3840&q=75)

[View author's profile](https://finimize.com/experts/newsroom "View author's profile")

[Finimize Newsroom](https://finimize.com/experts/newsroom "View author's profile")

2 mins

**What's going on here?**

Asia’s markets have a busy week starting April 27, with China’s factory surveys, Japan’s central-bank decision, and Australia’s [inflation](https://finimize.com/glossary/inflation) reading all landing as energy and trade uncertainty stays in focus.

**What does this mean?**

China kicked things off with a punchy number: industrial [profits](https://finimize.com/glossary/profit) rose 15.5% in the first quarter from a year earlier, helped by mining, manufacturing, tech, and chemicals. But investors are more interested in what’s next, and ING expects April’s purchasing managers’ index surveys – snapshots of business activity – to slip back just under 50, which signals mild contraction. Japan could be the bigger swing. Markets largely expect the Bank..

of Japan, the country’s central bank, to hold rates around 0.75%, but a hotter Tokyo [inflation](https://finimize.com/glossary/inflation) print – pushed up by energy costs, a weaker yen, and firmer wage growth – could revive talk of another hike. In Australia, the March-quarter [consumer](https://finimize.com/glossary/consumer) price index is the last major clue before the Reserve Bank of Australia’s May meeting, with Westpac looking for 4.2% inflation year-on-year.

**Why should I care?**

_**For markets:**_ _Rate expectations can change fast._

If Japan’s inflation surprises to the upside and the Bank of Japan sounds less patient, Japanese government [bond](https://finimize.com/glossary/bond) yields and the yen could jump – and that can ripple into global bond markets, because Japan is a huge source of capital. Australia’s inflation print matters for the same reason: a firm reading would keep “higher for longer” rate pricing in play. And if China’s activity surveys hover around 50 despite the [profit](https://finimize.com/glossary/profit) pop, investors may lean back toward policy-support hopes, reshuffling leadership between exporters, banks, and domestically focused stocks.

_**The bigger picture:**_ _Asia’s resilience is being put on trial._

This week’s releases also act like a supply-chain and pricing checkup. Stronger [export](https://finimize.com/glossary/export) and manufacturing numbers in parts of the region would point to a tech-led trade cycle still doing the heavy lifting. But any broad pickup in producer prices – the costs businesses pay – would suggest inflation pressure is sneaking back in through energy and imports. The mix matters: growth driven by real demand is sturdier than growth that’s mainly about higher prices.

At this stage, it appears that the Reserve Bank of Australia’s policy rate-setting board is mulling the merits of a further rate increase when it meets in early May.

Clearly, next week’s March consumer price index report will be critical for evaluating the likelihood of a further increase in Australia’s policy rate.

The US Federal Reserve faces a somewhat different decision than the RBA.

![](https://static.ffx.io/images/$zoom_0.378%2C$multiply_3%2C$ratio_1.5%2C$width_756%2C$x_0%2C$y_0/t_crop_custom/c_scale%2Cw_620%2Cq_88%2Cf_auto/7429c4e8c3ef6a231712c3aaa3637b56d2fd588f)

Federal Reserve chairman nominee Kevin Warsh argues that investment has wrought a productivity dividend that (other things equal) has raised the US economy’s “speed limit”. Bloomberg

It is true that both central banks are facing challenges due to the surge in oil prices following the conflict in Iran.

However, in the absence of the sharp rise in oil prices, there would be an argument that the door is ever so slightly ajar for the Fed to cut the policy rate.

Advertisement

While it is true that the Fed’s favoured inflation measure, the core private consumption expenditures (PCE) price index, is stuck at 3 per cent (unchanged from a year ago), there were signs of disinflation beneath the surface before the surge in oil prices.

For example, the Dallas Fed’s trimmed-mean core PCE measure was 2.3 per cent in February, the lowest rate since August 2021, despite broad-based price pressures stemming from the Trump tariff agenda.

> “US productivity growth has averaged 1.7 per cent per annum since the end of 2021. The equivalent Australian figure is -1 per cent.”

Federal Reserve Chair [designate](https://www.afr.com/world/north-america/trump-threatens-to-fire-federal-reserve-s-powell-if-he-does-not-leave-20260416-p5zo9o), Kevin Warsh, conjectures that disinflation in the US (of which there are glimpses in the Dallas Fed measure) will follow from tremendous (largely AI motivated) investment. In Warsh’s view, that investment has wrought a productivity dividend that (other things equal) has raised the US economy’s “speed limit”. In other words, the US economy can grow at a faster rate before igniting inflationary pressures.

That is a credible - if eminently debatable - position.

That is what leaves the door ever so slightly ajar for policy interest rate reductions in the US at some stage in 2026.

Advertisement

Having said all that, a policy rate reduction when the Fed meets next week is a remote prospect.

Judging by their commentary, most members of the Fed’s rate-setting Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) remain concerned that the recent [oil price surge](https://www.afr.com/markets/equity-markets/prepare-for-the-mother-of-all-default-cycles-20260410-p5zmvj) could unanchor inflation expectations. The RBA is similarly concerned.

There is an argument that a surge in oil prices is ultimately disinflationary. The narrative goes that the diminution in economic activity growth wrought by an oil price surge is of greater consequence than the price effects. Therefore, the bigger risk is that the economy is tipped into recession, which would ultimately be disinflationary.

That “disinflation” narrative was in the ascendant during the 1990s and into the early 2000s (probably right up to the onset of the pandemic). However, during that period, a number of structural forces suppressed inflation.

It may be less true in 2026.

I have previously instanced that the globalisation of labour supply (after the fall of the Berlin Wall and the “export” of labour from large emerging market economies such as China and India) is abating; globalisation of goods markets is in retreat as governments everywhere introduce protectionist measures; domestic regulation of goods and labour markets is increasing in scope leading to loss of flexibility in markets and attendant upward price pressures or “sticky” inflation; and baby boomer workforce participation is declining (limiting labour supply and lifting wages).

Advertisement

But what the US has going for it is that the surge in productivity is a structural disinflationary force not evident elsewhere, including in Australia.

US productivity growth has averaged 1.7 per cent per annum since the end of 2021. The equivalent Australian figure is -1 per cent. To put it more starkly, US productivity has grown by almost 7 per cent in that time; Australia’s productivity has fallen by 4 per cent.

That arguably puts the Fed in a better position than the RBA to “look through” any inflationary effects from oil prices.

So while I suspect that the Fed will eschew a policy rate reduction next week, it may well enact one at some stage this year, particularly if Warsh’s thesis plays out.

Warsh this space.

# Westpac hikes interest rates for the second time in weeks as RBA decision looms: 'Doubling down'

Banks are lifting interest rates ahead of the Reserve Bank's upcoming meeting on May 5, where it is expected to hike the cash rate once again.

[![Tamika Seeto](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/Gs9Sk0KFvCt6OD6W6vyIbw--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTgwO2g9ODA7Y2Y9d2VicA--/https://s.yimg.com/os/creatr-uploaded-images/2022-10/26817eb0-4b49-11ed-b3a7-71dcf909a946)](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/author/tamika-seeto)

[Tamika Seeto](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/author/tamika-seeto)· Finance Reporter

Updated Thu 23 April 2026 at 12:11 am GMT-42 min read

- [WBC.AX \\
  -0.08%](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/WBC.AX/ "WBC.AX")

- [CBA.AX \\
  -0.81%](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/CBA.AX/ "CBA.AX")

- [NAB.AX \\
+0.30%](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/NAB.AX/ "NAB.AX")


![Westpac](https://s.yimg.com/ny/api/res/1.2/c6FLtF.F2kyswvpJELpoaw--/YXBwaWQ9aGlnaGxhbmRlcjt3PTk2MDtoPTUxNztjZj13ZWJw/https://s.yimg.com/os/creatr-uploaded-images/2026-04/b0a6c710-3ec7-11f1-9ff7-3a666c6f9b39)

Westpac has hiked its fixed rates by 0.15 percentage points today, marking the second increase in three weeks. _(Source: Getty)_

[Westpac](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/WBC.AX/) has hiked its fixed home loan interest rates for the second time in three weeks. Aussie banks are pricing in further interest rate hikes from the [Reserve Bank of Australia](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/aussies-spend-more-as-fuel-crisis-pushes-up-prices-leaving-rba-in-really-hard-situation-024706047.html), with Westpac predicting borrowers could be dealt a [triple rate hike blow](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/westpac-warning-over-triple-interest-rate-blow-coming-for-aussie-households-tough-221708786.html).

Westpac increased its fixed rates by 0.15 percentage points today, adding to the bank’s [previous round of increases](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/westpac-joins-cba-nab-and-anz-in-hiking-fixed-interest-rates-as-triple-mortgage-blow-predicted-215628509.html) of up to 0.45 percentage points earlier this month. The Big Four bank’s lowest fixed rate is now 6.29 per cent on a two-year term.

It joins a spate of other lenders who have begun aggressively hiking fixed rates ahead of the RBA’s May meeting, including fellow major banks [Commonwealth Bank](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/CBA.AX/), [NAB](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/NAB.AX/) and [ANZ](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/quote/ANZ.AX/).

**RELATED**

- [**Aussies spend more as fuel crisis pushes up prices, leaving RBA in 'really hard situation'**](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/aussies-spend-more-as-fuel-crisis-pushes-up-prices-leaving-rba-in-really-hard-situation-024706047.html)

- [**Bunnings move to slash electricity bills for Aussie households**](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/bunnings-move-to-slash-electricity-bills-for-aussie-households-meaningful-savings-234647404.html)

- [**Warning as major change to cost of electricity grid to hit solar and battery users**](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/homeowners-warned-as-major-change-to-cost-of-electricity-grid-to-hit-solar-and-battery-users-223200341.html)


Canstar data insights director Sally Tindall said Westpac was “doubling down on fixed rate hikes” and it wasn’t an isolated event.

“Plenty of banks are heading back to the pricing drawing board, some for the second time in a matter of weeks, as a result of growing concern Australia’s inflation rate is about to spike once again,” Tindall said.

“Canstar analysis shows more than 90 per cent of lenders have [moved fixed rates](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/commonwealth-bank-hikes-fixed-home-loan-interest-rates-as-mortgage-holders-face-big-decision-041953403.html) since the last RBA decision, including all four big banks, with both Westpac and NAB having two stabs at it in this time.”

Banks aren’t just hiking the standard 0.25 per cent cash rate rise, either, with the average fixed rate rising by 0.43 percentage points since the last RBA decision in March.

Tindall said this suggested banks were bracing for “more than one further cash rate hike”.

“For borrowers, this swift repricing is a clear signal – opportunities to lock in a more competitive fixed rate are slipping away," she said.

Banks have also been tinkering with [term deposit rates](https://au.finance.yahoo.com/news/anz-macquarie-bank-hike-term-deposit-interest-rates-as-banks-rule-out-rba-rate-cut-235408337.html), with Macquarie making a 5 basis point increase to its nine-month rates and ANZ making a 25 basis point increase to its 12-month rate.

Westpac expects the RBA will hike rates again in May, June and August, which would take the cash rate to 4.85 per cent following two earlier hikes this year. This would be the highest the cash rate has been since November 2008.

The rest of the major banks are also expecting another hike in May.

# Big four bank hikes interest rates as inflation surges - as ominous Xmas warning is issued

Story by Sarah Brookes For Daily Mail Australia

• 4d
•

3 min read

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

- **RBA tipped for more rate hikes soon**
- **Economists warn of mortgage pain ahead**

One of Australia's big four banks has hiked its fixed rate home loans just weeks after forecasting interest rates could hit the highest level since the Global Financial Crisis.

It comes as the Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) is taking aggressive action to beat down inflation.

[Westpac](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/money/westpac/index.html) announced its fixed rates for home loans to owner-occupiers and investors would increase by 0.15 per cent in a message sent to customers on Thursday.

'This change reflects the increased cost of fixed rate funding and ensures our fixed home loan rates remain aligned with current market conditions,' the message read.

The rate increase will apply to all new fixed loans from today.

Westpac's figures show its lowest fixed rate is now 6.39 per cent.

Last month, the banking giant tipped the RBA would deliver three further rate hikes in 2026, adding hundreds of dollars to the average mortgage repayment as Middle East tensions fuel [inflationary](https://www.dailymail.co.uk/news/inflation/index.html) pressure.

The bank has forecast the RBA to increase the cash rate by 0.25 percentage points in May, June and August, making a total of five hikes in 2026.

The RBA has already lifted rates twice this year, in February and March, and borrowers are being warned to brace for another on May 5, when the RBA announces its next cash rate decision.

AMP economist Shane Oliver said a bout of stagflation - higher inflation and weaker growth - is already set in the near term even if there is a quick and sustained increase in ships flowing through the Strait of Hormuz.

'We expect the RBA to hike again but it’s a very close call,' he said.

'It can’t ignore the potential hit to economic growth, so the May RBA meeting we put the probability of a hike versus a hold at 60/40.'

Oliver said RBA Deputy Governor Andrew Hauser's comments at New York University last week, where he called for government support of the RBA’s unpopular rate hikes to curb inflation, could be interpreted as signalling another rate rise.

'Inflation expectations, wage pressures and cost and price pressures have all moved up since the War started and March data highlights that the labour market was tight going into the War, all of which biases the RBA towards more rate hikes,' he said.

'But against this, the labour market is a lagging indicator, and slumps in business and consumer confidence point to weaker economic conditions ahead which will dampen inflation eventually.

'In the near term we think the RBA will give primacy to the increased threat to inflation and so see another rate hike in May.'

During a fireside chat hosted by the Money Marketeers of NYU, Mr Hauser said Australia was heading for a 'big income shock' sparked by the escalating war in the Middle East.

He made it clear the RBA would not hesitate to keep lifting rates until inflation was beaten into submission.

'It is a central banker's nightmare,' he said.

'Rates will have to go to a level where they bring inflation back to target. And if that means them going higher, it means them going higher.'

Oxford Economics head of research Harry Murphy Cruise went even further, warning mortgage holders could be hit by five more interest rate hikes by Christmas if there was no quick resolution to the Iran war.

'Australia had an inflation problem well before this conflict kicked off,' he said.

'Households are in a really tough spot at the moment. They are seeing inflation rise around them.'

Headline inflation in Australia is currently 3.7 per cent and the RBA wants to keep inflation averaging between two and three per cent.

Most economists expect the RBA to lift rates to 4.35 per cent when it meets in May, while Westpac expects rates to be hiked again in June and August.

Westpac declined to comment.

# SEA leads fintech density as Asia gap widens

Story by Staff Reporter

• 1d
•

1 min read

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

**Singapore recorded 619 fintech companies per 1 million people.**

Southeast Asia (SEA) is the most [financial technology](https://asianbankingandfinance.net/financial-technology)-dense sub-region in Asia, with Singapore leading all markets.

SEA holds an average of 14 firms per one million people, according to a study by Singapore-based [UnaFinancial](https://asianbankingandfinance.net/company/unafinancial).

The study analysed 19 economies across Southeast Asia, East Asia, South Asia and Central Asia using a per-capita measure to compare fintech density.

Singapore leads the region with 619 fintech companies per million people.

South Asia ranked second with an average of 9.0 fintech firms per million people, largely driven by India at 11.1.

Central Asia and East Asia followed with averages of 5.8 and 4.8, respectively.

The report noted that Central Asia is expanding from a lower base. Kazakhstan’s fintech sector grew from about 50 companies in 2018 to 200 in 2024, whilst Uzbekistan increased from 24 to 103 over the same period.

Uzbekistan has also produced its first technology unicorn and is rolling out a fintech development strategy for 2025 to 2030.

UnaFinancial said there is a gap of more than 300 times between the most and least dense fintech markets in Asia, indicating uneven development across the region.

It added that improvements in digital infrastructure and access to capital could support faster growth in lower-density markets.

The study used fintech company data from Tracxn, along with figures from InvestHK, industry reports and national regulators.

Population estimates were based on World Bank data for 2024, and regional averages were calculated on a population-weighted basis.
